content.text: LangSmith is Anthropic-independent observability tooling built by LangChain. When tracing is enabled, every invocation of a LangChain chain, LLM call, tool run, or retriever is automatically recorded to a hosted LangSmith project. Each recorded invocation is a run — runs nest into traces — and traces are grouped into projects. The result is a complete audit trail of what your agent did, what it was given, what it returned, and how long each step took.

attrs.alt: LangSmith tracing architecture: runs from chains, LLMs, and tools are sent asynchronously to LangSmith and organized as traces within a project

content.text: Set two environment variables before importing LangChain. Every subsequent chain, LLM, tool, and retriever call in that process will be traced automatically — no code changes required.

export LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true
export LANGCHAIN_API_KEY="ls__..."
export LANGCHAIN_PROJECT="my-agent"   # optional; defaults to "default"
In Python code, set the variables before the first LangChain import, or use

import os
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2"] = "true"
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_API_KEY"] = "ls__..."
os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"] = "production-agent"

# All calls below are now traced
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Answer: {question}")
chain = prompt | llm

result = chain.invoke({"question": "What is LangSmith?"})
# A trace is now visible in your LangSmith project
tracing is non-blocking — LangSmith sends run data to its API in background threads. Your application's latency is not meaningfully increased by tracing.
call is a trace. Within that trace, every sub-step (LLM call, tool call, retriever call) is a child run. The full hierarchy is: Project → Trace → Run → Child Runs.
Logical grouping of related traces (one app or experiment)
One top-level invocation (one user request, one pipeline run)
One step within a trace (LLM, chain, tool, retriever)
Sub-step within a run (e.g., LLM call inside a chain)
Attach tags and metadata to a run by passing them in the
dict. Tags appear as filterable labels in the LangSmith UI; metadata is arbitrary key-value pairs stored alongside the run.

to record it as a run in LangSmith, even if it does not use LangChain internals. This is useful for custom preprocessing steps, postprocessing, or helper functions that you want to see in the trace tree.
Inspecting Traces in the LangSmith UI
The LangSmith web UI at smith.langchain.com shows every trace in your project. Key features for debugging agent workflows:
Run tree: hierarchical view of every step — click any run to see its inputs, outputs, and timing.
Token usage: total input/output tokens and cost per LLM run.
Latency breakdown: see which step is the bottleneck.
Feedback: add human ratings directly on a run (thumbs up/down, score) to build evaluation datasets.
Share: generate a shareable link to a specific trace for team review.
Querying Traces with the Python SDK
The LangSmith Python client lets you query traces programmatically — useful for building automated evaluations or extracting data for fine-tuning.

from langsmith import Client

client = Client()

# List recent runs in a project
runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production-agent",
    run_type="llm",           # only LLM calls
    error=False,              # only successful runs
    limit=100,
))

for run in runs[:5]:
    print(f"Run: {run.id}")
    print(f"  Model: {run.extra.get('invocation_params', {}).get('model_name', '?')}")
    print(f"  Latency: {run.end_time - run.start_time}")
    print(f"  Tokens: {run.prompt_tokens} in / {run.completion_tokens} out")
    print()

# Filter by metadata
filtered = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production-agent",
    filter='and(has(metadata, '{"user_id": "user-123"}')',
))
print(f"Runs for user-123: {len(filtered)}")
Tracing adds network calls even in development. Disable it in unit tests by unsetting the environment variable or using the context manager:

import os
import pytest
from langsmith.run_helpers import tracing_context

# Option 1: env var in test fixtures
@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def disable_tracing(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.delenv("LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2", raising=False)

# Option 2: context manager for a specific block
def test_chain_output():
    with tracing_context(enabled=False):
        result = chain.invoke({"question": "test"})
    assert result.content

# Option 3: global for all tests
os.environ.pop("LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2", None)

LangGraph graphs are automatically traced when tracing is enabled — every node execution appears as a child run under the graph's top-level trace. You can attach additional metadata to individual runs by reading the
that LangGraph passes to each node and forwarding it to nested LLM or tool calls.

from langsmith import traceable
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableConfig
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
from typing_extensions import TypedDict

class State(TypedDict):
    query: str
    answer: str

def call_model(state: State, config: RunnableConfig) -> dict:
    # config carries the run_id and tags from the parent graph trace
    # Pass it through to any nested calls so they nest under the right trace
    result = llm.invoke(state["query"], config=config)
    return {"answer": result.content}

graph = StateGraph(State)
graph.add_node("call_model", call_model)
graph.add_edge(START, "call_model")
graph.add_edge("call_model", END)
app = graph.compile()

# Tags set here flow through to every child run in the graph
result = app.invoke(
    {"query": "Explain quantum entanglement briefly"},
    config={"tags": ["production", "v2"], "metadata": {"user_id": "u-8841"}},
)

LangSmith lets you attach scores, comments, and corrections to a run after the fact. This is useful for logging thumbs-up/thumbs-down signals from users, recording human-reviewed correctness scores, or capturing golden-answer corrections for later fine-tuning. Use the

from langsmith import Client
import uuid

client = Client()

# Run the app, capturing the run_id from the output config
run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
result = app.invoke(
    {"query": "Capital of Japan?"},
    config={"run_id": run_id},
)

# User presses thumbs-up — log positive feedback
client.create_feedback(
    run_id=run_id,
    key="user_rating",
    score=1,          # 1 = positive, 0 = negative
    comment="Answered correctly and concisely",
)

# Record a correction (the reference answer) for evaluation
client.create_feedback(
    run_id=run_id,
    key="correction",
    value={"answer": "Tokyo"},
    comment="Ground truth for fine-tuning dataset",
)
run_id must be set before the call:
in the config before invoking the graph. If you forget, retrieve the run_id by querying the trace:

A LangSmith dataset is a collection of input/output examples you curate for offline evaluation. You can populate it from production traces (one-click in the UI or programmatically), then run an evaluator over every example to measure quality as your prompt or model changes.

from langsmith import Client
from langsmith.evaluation import evaluate

client = Client()

# Create a dataset (idempotent — does nothing if it already exists)
dataset_name = "capital-city-qa"
if not client.has_dataset(dataset_name=dataset_name):
    ds = client.create_dataset(dataset_name)
    client.create_examples(
        inputs=[
            {"query": "Capital of France?"},
            {"query": "Capital of Japan?"},
            {"query": "Capital of Brazil?"},
        ],
        outputs=[
            {"answer": "Paris"},
            {"answer": "Tokyo"},
            {"answer": "Brasília"},
        ],
        dataset_id=ds.id,
    )

# Define an evaluator function
def exact_match(run, example):
    predicted = (run.outputs or {}).get("answer", "").strip().lower()
    expected = (example.outputs or {}).get("answer", "").strip().lower()
    return {"key": "exact_match", "score": int(predicted == expected)}

# Run the evaluation — calls app.invoke for every example
results = evaluate(
    lambda x: app.invoke(x),   # the function under test
    data=dataset_name,
    evaluators=[exact_match],
    experiment_prefix="gpt-4o-mini-baseline",
)
print(results.to_pandas()[["inputs", "reference.answer", "outputs", "feedback.exact_match"]])

twice with different prompts or models, LangSmith records both as separate experiments under the same dataset. The Experiments tab in the UI shows a side-by-side comparison with score distributions, latency histograms, and per-example diffs. You can also compare programmatically.

from langsmith import Client

client = Client()

# List all experiments on a dataset
experiments = list(client.list_projects(reference_dataset_name="capital-city-qa"))
for exp in experiments:
    runs = list(client.list_runs(project_name=exp.name, run_type="chain"))
    scores = []
    for r in runs:
        fb = list(client.list_feedback(run_ids=[str(r.id)], feedback_key="exact_match"))
        if fb:
            scores.append(fb[0].score)
    avg = sum(scores) / len(scores) if scores else 0
    print(f"{exp.name}: avg exact_match={avg:.2f} over {len(scores)} examples")

Every LangChain and LangGraph trace that involves an LLM call automatically records token counts and timing. LangSmith's Monitoring dashboard aggregates this data into time-series charts for p50/p95 latency, total tokens, and estimated cost (based on the model's published pricing). You can also query these metrics programmatically.

from langsmith import Client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

client = Client()

# Fetch runs from the last 24 hours
cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=24)
runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="my-agent-project",
    run_type="llm",           # only LLM calls, not chain wrappers
    start_time=cutoff,
))

total_tokens = sum((r.total_tokens or 0) for r in runs)
latencies = [(r.end_time - r.start_time).total_seconds()
             for r in runs if r.end_time and r.start_time]
p95 = sorted(latencies)[int(len(latencies) * 0.95)] if latencies else 0

print(f"Last 24h: {len(runs)} LLM calls")
print(f"Total tokens: {total_tokens:,}")
print(f"p95 latency: {p95:.2f}s")

decorator works recursively. If a traceable function calls another traceable function, the child appears as a nested run in the same trace. This is useful for breaking a complex pipeline into named stages that each show up separately in the run tree.
for external API calls. The run_type controls which icon appears in the UI and which metrics are collected.
Pass tags=[] and metadata={} in RunnableConfig
Create examples from UI or client.create_examples()
Unit tests should not send data to LangSmith. Disable tracing in your pytest fixture so tests run offline and don't pollute production project data.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def disable_langsmith(monkeypatch):
    """Disable LangSmith tracing for all tests in this module."""
    monkeypatch.delenv("LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2", raising=False)
    monkeypatch.delenv("LANGCHAIN_API_KEY", raising=False)
    yield

def test_rag_returns_answer():
    result = rag("What is RAG?")
    assert isinstance(result, str)
    assert len(result) > 10

# For integration tests that DO want to trace (e.g., capturing golden outputs),
# use a dedicated test project so results don't mix with production:
@pytest.fixture
def test_tracing_config():
    import os
    os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"] = "test-suite"
    yield
    del os.environ["LANGCHAIN_PROJECT"]
Enable tracing with LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true and LANGCHAIN_API_KEY — all LangChain calls are then traced automatically.
Traces are organized as Project -> Trace -> Run -> Child Runs. Each run records inputs, outputs, latency, and token usage.
Pass tags and metadata in RunnableConfig to annotate runs for filtering in the LangSmith UI.
Decorate any Python function with @traceable to include custom steps in the trace tree.
Query traces programmatically with the LangSmith Python client for evaluation and data extraction.
Disable tracing in tests with monkeypatch.delenv or the tracing_context(enabled=False) context manager.
